Key activities: Identify and prioritise disease-relevant genes, proteins, RNAs, pathways, networks and biomarkers using genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, phenotypic data, literature mining, knowledge graphs and bioinformatics; assess causal evidence, tractability or druggability, unmet clinical need, indication fit, competitive landscape and target product profile alignment.
Comments: Target-to-hit cycles are ~12 months; target validation programmes up to $150k; early investment before a “No Go” decision can exceed $0.9M.

Key activities: Validate that modulating the target changes disease biology using CRISPR, gene knockdown or knockout, RNAi, antibodies, affinity reagents, chemical probes and perturbation assays; confirm mechanism, causality, selectivity, on-target and off-target effects, translational biomarkers and human disease relevance.
Comments: Typical target-validation costs are about $268k and may reach about $0.94M if started at risk.

Key activities: Design, miniaturise and validate biochemical, biophysical and cell-based assays for screening and follow-up; optimise controls, signal window, reproducibility, Z’-factor, automation readiness, reagent stability, image-analysis pipelines and data quality control.
Comments: Often part of the 12-month target-to-hit phase; hit-identification programmes funded up to $300k for 18 months.

Key activities: Discover initial actives via high-throughput screening, focused libraries, fragment screening, DNA-encoded libraries, virtual screening and structure-based screening; prioritise hits by potency, novelty, selectivity, tractability, assay interference risk and confirmatory retesting.
Comments: Identifying actives takes about 12 months and may cost $0.47-1.46M; accelerator programs allocate up to $300k.

Key activities: Re-synthesise and triage hits, confirm activity in orthogonal assays and run design-make-test-analyse cycles to improve potency, selectivity, liabilities and early ADME; remove false positives, PAINS, reactive chemotypes and unstable series.
Comments: Accelerated programmes can take 6-9 months; confirmation of hits can take about 18 months; hit-to-lead funding up to $500k.

Key activities: Select lead series with defined structure-activity relationships, synthetic feasibility, developability and early in vivo signal; compare backup series, establish PK/PD hypotheses, early safety margins and route or scaffold attractiveness for progression.
Comments: Identifying a chemical lead costs about $0.35M; cumulative investment to this point can reach $2.1M.

Key activities: Iteratively optimise leads for potency, selectivity, exposure, solubility, permeability, metabolic stability, DMPK, safety and formulation compatibility using medicinal chemistry, computational design, active learning and multi-parameter optimisation.
Comments: Lead optimisation often lasts 12-15 months and may extend to 36 months; accelerator budgets up to $1M.

Key activities: Nominate a development candidate and pre-formulation package by characterising solid state, salt or co-crystal options, physicochemical stability, excipient compatibility, bioavailability, dose feasibility and route of administration; define the initial dosage form and CMC-enabling requirements.
Comments: Selection of a development candidate and pre-IND meeting preparation cost about $0.275-0.37M.

Key activities: Characterise absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion and pharmacodynamics using in vitro and in vivo studies; quantify exposure, bioavailability, clearance, metabolite profile, drug-drug interaction risk, transporter effects and PK/PD relationships.
Comments: Preclinical programmes last about 1-2 years; pharmacology studies alone cost about $1-2M.

Key activities: Run GLP toxicology, safety pharmacology and genotoxicity programmes including acute, repeat-dose, reproductive, carcinogenicity and local tolerance assessments; determine NOAEL, target-organ toxicity, exposure margins, species selection and risk mitigation.
Comments: Acute studies last days to 2 weeks, subchronic 90 days to 6 months, chronic 6-24 months; toxicology studies account for about $1.8M of preclinical spend, and full preclinical programmes can cost up to $10M.

Key activities: Demonstrate therapeutic effect and dose-response in disease-relevant in vitro, ex vivo and in vivo models; define translatability, biomarker strategy, responder hypotheses and comparative efficacy versus standard of care or controls.
Comments: IND-enabling assay development and study durations often total 6-12 months; pharmacology and efficacy studies form part of the $6-8M preclinical budget.

Key activities: Conduct optional human microdosing or exploratory IND studies to obtain very early human PK, biodistribution or target engagement signals with sub-therapeutic exposure; de-risk formulation, analytics and candidate selection.
Comments: Microdose programmes cost substantially less than full Phase I studies.

Key activities: Improve solubility, dissolution, stability, release profile and delivery route using salt screening, particle engineering, excipient selection, nano or micro-formulations and device or parenteral delivery design; optimise manufacturability and clinical usability.
Comments: Usually concurrent with candidate selection; pre-formulation costs about $0.28-0.37M; formulation and manufacturing to clinic may add about $3.8M.

Key activities: Assemble IND-enabling nonclinical, CMC, pharmacology, toxicology and bioanalytical packages; prepare investigator brochures, clinical-start protocols, safety summaries, module-ready content and regulatory correspondence for IND submission.
Comments: IND-enabling programmes cost $2-7M for small molecules.

Key activities: Design clinical protocols, endpoints, eligibility criteria, randomisation, dose escalation, sample schedules and bioanalytical plans; select sites, activate study systems, train investigators, obtain ethics approvals and ensure GCP and patient protection.
Comments: Per study site; activating a trial site costs about $20-25k.

Key activities: Run first-in-human, SAD, MAD, food-effect, DDI or special-population studies to assess safety, tolerability, PK, PD and maximum tolerated or biologically active dose; review adverse events and dose-escalation decisions.
Comments: Per-patient costs yield $4-5.26M total; some estimates place Phase I spending at about $25M and $28M on average.

Key activities: Evaluate preliminary efficacy, dose-response, regimen selection and proof-of-concept in patients; monitor adverse events, protocol deviations, subgroup signals and biomarkers while refining endpoints and go or no-go criteria.
Comments: Phase II trials cost $7-20M in some sources; other studies report about $58.6M or about $65M.

Key activities: Confirm efficacy and safety in pivotal, randomised, often double-blind multicentre trials; manage recruitment, site performance, adjudication, data cleaning, statistical analysis and regulatory-readiness of the clinical package.
Comments: Reported costs range from $20-100M+ to about $350M; average spend reported around $282M or $255M.

Key activities: Monitor long-term safety, effectiveness, adherence and real-world outcomes after approval; generate RWE, post-authorisation safety or efficacy studies, comparative effectiveness data and evidence for line extensions or label changes.
Comments: Ongoing for years to decades after approval; post-marketing surveillance programmes may cost $20-300M.

Key activities: Assemble and publish the final regulatory dossier including clinical, nonclinical, CMC, labeling, risk management and pharmacovigilance components; manage eCTD publishing, QC, submission tracking and health authority questions.
Comments: Time to compile and submit; application fees for NDAs/BLAs range from $2.34M to $4.68M.

Key activities: Support regulatory review through information requests, inspection readiness, advisory committee preparation, label negotiations, post-marketing commitments and responses to complete response or approval actions.
Comments: Standard review is about 10 months; priority review about 6 months; review funded by PDUFA fees included in the NDA/BLA user fee.

Key activities: Analyse attrition drivers such as lack of efficacy, safety or toxicity, PK or PD failure, trial execution problems, CMC or manufacturing deficiencies, regulatory objections, competitive shifts and commercial non-viability.
Comments: No quantitative range provided.

Key activities: Prepare abbreviated submissions demonstrating pharmaceutical equivalence, bioequivalence, sameness where required, CMC quality and manufacturing compliance; manage BE studies, labeling carve-outs, deficiency responses and GDUFA workflows.
Comments: Median approval time is about 35 months; GDUFA ANDA fee is about $0.358M with additional facility fees; total development costs are usually several million.

Key activities: Collect and analyse adverse event reports through systems like FAERS; implement risk mitigation and label updates. Includes Drug Safety, ICSR case processing, signal detection and signal management, benefit-risk assessments, RMP or PSMF maintenance, E2B(R3) compliance, MedDRA coding, SUSAR reporting, QPPV services, literature surveillance, label monitoring, and EudraVigilance gateway management.
Comments: Ongoing throughout product life; post-marketing surveillance programmes cost $20-300M.

Key activities: Extend lifecycle value through new indications, line extensions, formulations, combination products or special populations; run supplemental clinical studies, generate supporting evidence and manage sNDA, sBLA or variation filings.
Comments: New studies may take about 1-2 years and FDA review about 10 months; additional clinical trials for new indications generally cost tens of millions, using Phase II/III cost ranges.

Key activities: Scale process from pilot to commercial manufacture with tech transfer, process validation, analytical methods, comparability, CMC control strategy, deviation or CAPA management, batch release and cGMP quality oversight.
Comments: Setting up a compliant pharmaceutical manufacturing facility costs around $2M; large-scale commercial facilities may cost much more.
